item.author: Zeteo

item.title: Unhinged Trump Threatens to Jail Reporters, Media Elite Can’t Wait to Party With Him
item.description: Good evening, and welcome to this week’s ‘Ragebait.’ Justin Baragona here, bringing you the latest media dispatches from the right-wing industrial outrage complex and Bari World. In tonight’s edition, we take issue with the establishment press delivering a collective shrug at the president’s…
item.author: Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
Good evening, and welcome to this week’s ‘Ragebait.’

Justin Baragona here, bringing you the latest media dispatches from the right-wing industrial outrage complex and Bari World. In tonight’s edition, we take issue with the establishment press delivering a collective shrug at the president’s increasingly deranged threats towards free speech – while they simultaneously prepare to “honor” him. Plus, we look into who Bari Weiss is wining and dining, and share the world’s worst history lesson delivered by Trump’s Fox brain trust.

Trump Ramps Up His War on the Press

Within the span of roughly 30 hours, Donald Trump threatened to jail journalists if they didn’t burn their confidential sources and accused a news network of criminal conduct for publishing an accurate public statement. And in both cases, he called for the feds to criminally investigate media outlets.

Yet, despite the media-bashing president ramping up his already white-hot incitement towards the fourth estate to new levels, the White House press corps tasked to cover him has delivered a collective shrug.

And all the while, the elite political media class is prepping to spend a night publicly feting this man during a lavish party. But don’t worry, because in an act of unparalleled bravery, they’ll make sure to show the president their defiant support for the First Amendment by wearing matching accessories.

During a Monday afternoon press conference, Trump raged about reports on a missing airman following the downing of an American jet fighter in Iran on Friday. Though the pilot was recovered within several hours, the “back-seater” was stranded until Sunday, when he was rescued during a daring mission.

“We’re looking very hard to find that leaker,” Trump fumed. “They didn’t know there was somebody missing until this leaker gave the information. So whoever it was, we think we’ll be able to find it out because we’re going to go to the media company that released it, and we’re going to say, national security, give it up or go to jail.”

Calling the source a “sick person,” Trump growled that a journalist at an unspecified news outlet that reported on the missing airman needed to give up their source’s name. “And the person that did the story will go to jail if he doesn’t say,” the president exclaimed. “They put this mission at great risk.”

While Trump didn’t say who he was referring to, and the White House merely said an “investigation is underway” when reached for comment, sources familiar with the situation tell my Zeteo colleague Asawin Suebsaeng that top Trump officials have identified leaks on this matter to CBS, Axios, and the New York Times as part of an investigation.

These sources confirmed that those were at least some of the news outlets Trump referred to when he called for unnamed reporters to be jailed if they don’t cough up their sources to him.

Israeli journalist Amit Segal – who has boasted of his proximity to Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu – identified himself as the first to report on the missing airman, though he somewhat backpedaled when approached by the New York Post. “I’m not sure I was the first,” he said. “And anyway — I will protect my sources.” Barak Ravid, Segal’s Channel 12 colleague who also works for Axios, was also among the earliest to report on the search for the crew member.

With Trump outright calling for the imprisonment of their brethren, one would think that the press corps on hand for the president’s briefing would at least follow up and question him on his latest media threat. Yet, despite taking questions for roughly 40 minutes, Trump was not once asked about it.

On Tuesday, however, a reporter did confront acting Attorney General Todd Blanche on the president’s remarks, asking if there is an ongoing leak investigation. He said he wouldn’t comment on current investigations other than to say “we will always investigate those types of leaks,” which “means sending a subpoena to the reporter.”

While the DC reporting class – now full of compliant MAGA lickspittles – largely shrugged off Trump’s “give it up or go to jail” threats, at least some press freedom organizations called the president out – while also noting a bit of irony.

“In fact, Trump’s hatred of the press runs so deep that, in his haste to lock up journalists for doing their jobs, he may have inadvertently threatened Israeli journalists and officials who were reportedly behind the story with prison,” Freedom of the Press Foundation chief of advocacy Seth Stern said. “Anyone who for some reason considers taking Trump seriously next time he goes on a half-baked anti-press rant should remember that.”

Soon after declaring he had reached a two-week ceasefire pact with Iran just 90 minutes before his 8pm genocidal deadline, Trump apparently revealed he was fully unaware of what the Iranian government was actually saying when he accused CNN of peddling fake news.

Claiming that an “alleged Statement put out” by CNN was a “FRAUD,” adding that he’d already shared Iran’s “Official” statement from its foreign minister, Trump demanded an immediate retraction and apology from the network while also calling for a criminal probe.

“Authorities are looking to determine whether or not a crime was committed on the issuance of the Fake CNN World Statement, or was it a sick rogue player? CNN is being ordered to immediately withdraw this Statement with full apologies for their, as usual, terrible ‘reporting,’” Trump added in his Truth Social post. “Results of the investigation will be announced in the near future.”

The White House’s social media rapid response team echoed the president’s rant, stating that the “alleged statement by ‘Iran’ is a FRAUD” and for CNN to “Correct immediately!” Joining the pile on, Brendan Carr – Trump’s appointed attack dog at the FCC who has no authority over cable news – called for “change at CNN” because they were “pushing out a hoax headline in such a sensitive national security moment.”

The problem is, the statement CNN shared was from the Islamic Republic’s Supreme National Security Council and had already appeared on Iran’s state news agencies. And, in fact, several other mainstream media outlets – such as the New York Times – had also reported on the statement.

Of course, the security council’s response boasted that Iran had achieved a “great victory” over the US and forced Trump to accept its 10-point peace plan, which would include Iran’s continued control over the Strait of Hormuz and all sanctions lifted – showing that Trump is already losing the messaging war.

“The statement in question was obtained by CNN from Iranian officials and reported on multiple Iranian state media outlets,” a CNN spokesperson said on Tuesday night. “We received the statement from specific official Iranian spokespeople who are known to us.”

The White House did not respond to a request for comment about the validity of CNN’s reporting.

Meanwhile, we are just a few weeks out from journalists and executives at many of these news outlets that Trump is looking to criminally punish attending a party where the press-hating president will be “honored” by the White House Correspondents’ Association.

But don’t worry, folks. Some of those reporters at the black-tie affair are going to wear pocket squares and pins emblazoned with the words of the First Amendment.

That’ll show him!

Bari Wines and Dines Pete

Speaking of the White House Correspondents’ Dinner, not only are the major networks and legacy news organizations participating this year despite Trump’s attendance and continued demonization of the press, but they are also bringing the president’s lackeys as guests.

Our friend Lachlan Cartwright at Breaker reported this week that a number of Trump administration officials and allies will have featured seats at the tables of prominent news outlets.

It should come as no surprise to our readers who CBS News boss Bari Weiss has asked to join her at the shindig: Pete Hegseth – the Fox morning weekend host turned Christian nationalist war chief, whose vendetta against press freedoms rivals even his boss’s.

While it’s not clear yet if Hegseth has accepted Weiss’s request to be her plus-one (the Pentagon and CBS haven’t returned my requests for comment), the invite shouldn’t come as too much of a shock.

As we reported last month, Weiss has booked Hegseth for on-air sitdowns, even as rival networks and publications remain largely unified in not pursuing interviews with the defense secretary in protest of his media policy changes at the Pentagon.

Meanwhile, it isn’t just conservative-leaning organizations – such as CBS or Rupert Murdoch’s media empire – that are rolling out the welcome wagon to Team Trump. Mainstream outlets like Reuters and ABC News have also extended invitations to Trump officials, according to Cartwright.

There is one media company that has announced it will not participate in this farcical charade. Earlier this week, HuffPost declared that it would not attend the dinner and would instead provide real-time coverage and analysis of the event for its readers.

“HuffPost remains committed to delivering rigorous, independent reporting that holds those in power accountable,” the outlet said in a statement.

With a Brain Trust Like This…

Despite the last-minute two-week ceasefire being an obvious major win for Iran and already in danger of being blown apart, it was no surprise that Trump’s sycophants at Fox would proclaim that he had hit a “home run” with the deal.

Still, the president’s ‘Fox News Cabinet,’ led by shadow chief of staff Sean Hannity and “The Great One” Mark Levin, continued to serve as Trump’s wartime consigliere through his TV on Tuesday night. Besides calling for the president to snatch all the enriched uranium from Iran – something Trump parroted on Wednesday morning – the Fox hosts also urged him to foment a civil war by arming the Iranian population.

And the historic examples they used to support their case were beyond parody.

“We have to arm the people... Reagan did it in Afghanistan. He did it in Nicaragua. He did it in Honduras. He did it in Angola. Donald Trump can do exactly the same thing,” Levin exclaimed on Tuesday night.

“I think with the freedom fighters against the Contras, it worked. For the Mujahideen in Afghanistan, it helped beat the Soviet Union. I think it would be very effective,” Hannity responded.

Setting aside that apparently Hannity doesn’t realize that the Contras were the supposed “freedom fighters” in 1980s Nicaragua, do we really need to point out just how ridiculous these comparisons are? And how that all worked out in the long run?

Watch out for my newsletter, ‘Ragebait,’ every week. I focus on the right-wing outrage-industrial complex and the intersection of our political and media landscapes. Send any tips on Signal (justinbaragona.85).

Check out more from Zeteo:

item.title: I Watched Right-Wing Journalists Try to Shame Abdul El-Sayed and Hasan Piker
item.description: Hundreds of Michiganders waited in long lines in the cold for Senate candidate Abdul El-Sayed’s rallies with Hasan Piker at two universities. Zeteo was inside those rallies, where the national reporters who traveled to Michigan for the events seemed extremely puzzled as to why the progressive…
item.author: "Zeteo | U.S. Politics"
item.content:encoded:
Hundreds of Michiganders waited in long lines in the cold for Senate candidate Abdul El-Sayed’s rallies with Hasan Piker at two universities.

Zeteo was inside those rallies, where the national reporters who traveled to Michigan for the events seemed extremely puzzled as to why the progressive politician would choose to campaign with the popular leftist Twitch streamer – and they were intently focused on Israel.

“Hasan, do you feel like your presence on the campaign trail may hurt the candidates that you’re supporting more than help them?” a Fox News reporter asked. Another reporter asked El-Sayed what he would say to people who attend a local synagogue that was the site of a foiled terrorist attack, and are “concerned now about you campaigning with Hasan.”

Olivia Reingold, a writer for Bari Weiss’s Free Press who has defended Israel’s manufactured famine in Gaza, created a viral moment for El-Sayed when she asked him, “Does Israel have a right to exist as a Jewish state?”

“What do you mean by Jewish state?” El-Sayed responded. As Reingold stumbled and failed to answer, he retorted: “If you can’t define the question, I’m not going to answer your question.”

In recent weeks, a well-funded centrist think tank and El-Sayed’s Democratic opponents have led a campaign to try to publicly cancel Piker, and El-Sayed for rallying with him. And as President Donald Trump was actively threatening to commit genocide in Iran on Tuesday, the US media was busy attempting to shame El-Sayed over some of Piker’s past controversial comments. Perhaps unsurprisingly, the journalists filed stories with headlines focused on the Muslim men’s anti-Israel views and Piker’s old remarks.

“Michigan Senate candidate El-Sayed declines to disavow Hasan Piker’s past comments,” Politico wrote. Jewish Insider’s headline went further: “Abdul El-Sayed emphasizes anti-Israel rhetoric at rallies with antisemitic streamer Hasan Piker.” The Jewish Telegraphic Agency headlined its piece: “In rallies taking on Israel, a defiant Hasan Piker boosts Michigan Senate candidate Abdul el-Sayed.”

You would never know from the reports that El-Sayed’s rallies were, in fact, focused around affordability, getting money out of politics and into people’s pockets, and securing Medicare for All. Nor would you know that El-Sayed’s decision to invite Piker to his rallies at the University of Michigan and Michigan State University has been a big boon for his campaign.

El-Sayed’s communications director, Roxie Richner, told Zeteo that the campaign has seen an “increase in momentum” over the last month. It now has 4,000 volunteers, and the El-Sayed campaign rose to one the top five fundraisers on ActBlue the week after the Piker events were announced. Richner said over 1,000 people signed up for each rally, and both halls, which had over 500 seats, were filled to capacity.

‘I Go Everywhere’

El-Sayed is one of three Democrats running in a hotly contested primary that could determine the balance of power in the Senate. El-Sayed is the most progressive candidate in the primary – he is the only one in the race who has never taken corporate PAC cash, supports abolishing ICE, and champions Medicare for All. He has also been the most outspoken opponent of Israel’s genocide, and has vowed not to send any arms to Israel, including so-called defensive weapons. The genocide likely cost Democrats Michigan in 2024, a key swing state that is home to a sizeable Arab and Muslim population.

It makes sense why he would campaign with Piker. The 34-year-old Twitch streamer represents the left flank in the streaming space that is traditionally dominated by the right. Piker has amassed an audience of over 3 million followers, many of them young men, a key electorate that flocked to Trump in 2024.

On Tuesday, Piker’s nearly 10-hour stream of El-Sayed’s rallies and his day on the campaign trail drew over 1 million viewers.

Third Way, a billionaire-funded think tank, has spearheaded the cancellation campaign, and pressured El-Sayed to uninvite Piker from Tuesday’s rally. It must be noted the group has vowed to be the “chief opponent of the left” in the 2028 Democratic presidential primary and plans to spend $30 million to $50 million on this effort. Third Way has said it opposes candidates who support Medicare for All, the Green New Deal, abolishing ICE, or “open borders.”

Some establishment Democrats in Congress joined the chorus, with some pledging to Politico that they would never join Piker’s stream. Senator Cory Booker, one of the Democrats preemptively refusing to go on Piker’s show, claimed on ‘Pod Save America’ that he’d never actually heard of the streamer before the recent news cycle.

El-Sayed’s primary opponents, AIPAC-backed Rep. Haley Stevens and state Senator Mallory McMorrow, have also slammed El-Sayed for campaigning with Piker. McMorrow absurdly compared him to neo-Nazi streamer Nick Fuentes, citing controversial comments Piker made in the wake of the Hamas-led attacks on Israel and about Israel’s genocide in Gaza.

Speaking about his opponents, El-Sayed told Zeteo on Tuesday night, “They have a vision of the people they want to be talking to. Mine is a lot more expansive. I go everywhere and talk to everybody.”

In recent weeks, Piker has come under fire for having said during a 2019 livestream that “America deserved 9/11.” He later apologized for the comment and contended that he was referring to the concept of blowback, where US global intervention can lead to hostility from foreign groups.

But the main criticism levied against Piker is antisemitism. Piker once called Orthodox Jews in Israel “inbred.” He recently expressed regret for the comment, while noting he has also used that term as a general pejorative to describe neo-Nazis, white supremacists, and other groups.

Piker has defended himself as a fervent, consistent opponent of antisemitism and far-right fascism. El-Sayed has repeatedly said in recent weeks that he does not necessarily agree with everything the streamer has said but wants to connect with his audience and meet voters where they are.

‘Manufactured Controversy’

At both events, it was clear the cancellation campaign against Piker has failed.

While the national reporters filed stories about El-Sayed’s rhetoric on Israel and Piker’s past controversial comments, MSU’s student newspaper reported that most of the students they interviewed had attended the event because Piker was invited.

Some attendees similarly told Zeteo that Piker’s appearance was why they decided to come to a political rally on a Tuesday afternoon, and were willing to wait in long lines.

“Manufactured controversy” is how one attendee, Nick Coffin-Callis, described the recent anti-Piker campaign to Zeteo at Michigan State University ahead of the rally.

Standing outside to get into the rally at the University of Michigan, Dustin Krcatovich, an El-Sayed supporter, said that the Piker discourse was “preposterous but expected.”

“If somebody is reaching people to the degree that he is, people are going to try to shut him down if they’re in opposition of what he represents,” Krcatovich said. “I think if you listen to something that’s anything more than a 15-second soundbite… it’s pretty clear where his morals and ethics are.”

At the Ann Arbor campus, the line to get into the hall wrapped around the building, and people were eventually directed to an overflow area once it filled up.

El-Sayed, who has not bowed to pressure to sever ties with the streamer, told Zeteo on Tuesday night at the University of Michigan that his strategy to connect with young men in this campaign is to “go where they are,” while presenting a “benevolent” version of masculinity that counters the toxicity perpetuated by MAGA figureheads.

While Tuesday marked Piker’s first time stumping for a candidate, he’s been involved in political commentary for years, and Democrats have appeared on his stream. Piker was even invited to stream from the Democratic National Convention in 2024.

When Zeteo asked Piker about why Democrats have chosen this moment to fight about him, he said “they would rather talk about anything but the thing that many of them wanted, which is this war.”

But he said in the age of new media, voices like his can no longer be gatekept or cast aside, pointing to the “sea change” on Palestine.

Piker said the party’s “manufactured outrage” over him is rooted in fear, because establishment Democrats are losing their hold over the party, especially as public opinion shifts further away from them on key issues like abolishing ICE, stopping Israel’s genocide, and opposing the war in Iran.

“They are united in their hatred of not just me, but all of us, and I welcome their hatred,” Piker told the Ann Arbor crowd.

After the rally, Piker told Zeteo he’s supporting El-Sayed’s campaign because he is “exactly the change that I want to see in the Democratic Party, a party that is actually going to fight back against the excesses and the violence that the fascist Republicans represent.”

Other prominent figures on the left, including Senator Bernie Sanders, have refused to distance themselves from Piker.

Progressive Michigan Rep. Rashida Tlaib – a surprise guest at the Ann Arbor rally – took to the stage and told Piker he was “never going to get canceled up in Michigan.”

Tlaib told the crowd that El-Sayed is threatening to some because he represents a movement of people that are “sick and tired” of sellouts in Congress. “And let me tell you – all the haters can keep hating, because when I ran, I only won by 900 votes, and now they can’t get rid of me,” she said.

Rep. Summer Lee, who defeated an AIPAC-backed challenger in 2024 after criticizing Israel, drove down from her district in Pittsburgh to support El-Sayed alongside Piker.

“There’s a lot of people who did not want us to be here with y’all today, it was folks who don’t want us talking to people who might rock the boat,” she told a full house at the University of Michigan.

Speaking with Zeteo about the anti-Piker discourse, Lee said there are “concerted efforts to distract us from our attempts at building solidarity.”

When Zeteo asked El-Sayed why Democrats have chosen this moment to fight about Piker, he responded with a smile: “I’ll leave that to readers to surmise for themselves.”

Have a tip for Minnah? Reach out on Signal at minnahfarshad.94.

Check out more from Zeteo:

item.title: 'No Doubt War Will Resume': Skeptical Iranians Say It's Too Early to Celebrate
item.description: TEHRAN, Iran – The morning after US President Donald Trump announced a two-week ceasefire with Iran, the underlying mood here in Tehran was far from relief. As people continued their daily routines, as they had in the days before, a divide emerged in how Iranians were reacting to the US president’s…
item.author: Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
TEHRAN, Iran – The morning after US President Donald Trump announced a two-week ceasefire with Iran, the underlying mood here in Tehran was far from relief.

As people continued their daily routines, as they had in the days before, a divide emerged in how Iranians were reacting to the US president’s announcement. Many saw the ceasefire as a victory for Iran, while some thought their country had agreed to it too soon. Others remained deeply distrustful of the Americans, and many expressed an instinctive determination to keep pressure on both Washington and the country’s own leadership. There was, however, an overall unifying feeling of raw defiance among the Iranians I spoke to.

Ahmad, a 42-year-old bookseller, saw the pause as a sign of Trump’s humiliation, suggesting the failure to secure the Strait of Hormuz might cause Gulf states to reconsider supporting America.

Saeed, a 65-year-old taxi driver, viewed the ceasefire as a victory, comparing Trump’s actions to Saddam Hussein’s invasion in 1980, which led to a nearly eight-year war between the two countries. “They said Iran would surrender in three days,” Saeed exclaimed. “But now the Strait of Hormuz is in our hands.

Negin, a 27-year-old barista, argued the ceasefire came too soon, referring to the deaths of scores of schoolchildren in Minab. “After so much innocent blood, there should not have been a ceasefire this quickly,” she told me.

“I have no doubt the war will resume,” she added. “The US and Israel cannot be trusted.”

Meanwhile, Sobhan, a 56-year-old restaurant cleaner, believed the ceasefire was merely a prelude to renewed unrest and insisted people must maintain their presence in public spaces.

The tense morning could not be separated from the night that preceded it.

As the deadline for Trump’s threat to wipe out “a whole civilization” approached, some residents had prepared by storing water and charging power banks, while others attempted to leave the cities. But a significant number took a different path: they poured into the streets.

In cities including Ahvaz, capital of the southwestern province of Khuzestan, and Tehran, citizens formed human chains to protect the infrastructure that Trump was threatening to blow up. On Ahvaz’s White Bridge, hundreds linked hands across the Karun River in an act of defiance. Similar scenes unfolded near power stations and public squares in the capital. Participants described the gatherings not as spontaneous protests, but as deliberate attempts to project unity and send a message that the country would not be intimidated.

In the final hours leading up to Trump’s deadline, Tehran did not resemble a city on the brink of war. By 10pm – five and a half hours before the deadline – shops remained open. Large groups filled the streets in car convoys and on foot, planning to remain outside together until the 3:30am deadline passed.

Marzieh, a 35-year-old philosophy graduate, brought water, tea, and snacks to sit with her friends. “I feel safer here,” she said. “In the square, alongside my friends and the people of my country.” Her husband Kazem, a civil engineer, echoed the sentiment. “Whatever happens, I want to experience it alongside my people. These are moments you cannot miss.”

Enghelab Square in central Tehran, historically the focal point for major gatherings, was heavily crowded. Sara, 38, who recently returned after 15 years in Canada, told her friends she could not accept the humiliation of an ancient civilization being threatened by a newly established country. “Paying attention to Trump is itself a form of surrender,” she said. “The best response is to ignore his nonsense altogether.”

Around midnight, the sound of air defense systems and distant fighter jets echoed, but the crowd quickly drowned out the noise with chants of “Allahu Akbar.” Ali and Pourandokht, a couple aged 50 and 57, told me they had made no emergency preparations. Pointing out that electricity in their area had been targeted previously but restored within hours, they expressed confidence in the country’s institutions. “Taking him [Trump] seriously is the biggest mistake,” they said. “He is a deceitful opportunist.”

Sahar Daneshvar, a 40-year-old journalist and television presenter, stayed in Enghelab Square until 6:00am. She noted that crowds were significantly larger than on previous nights as people anticipated what Trump might do at the 3:30am mark.

As the deadline approached, “murmurs began to ripple through the crowd,” Daneshvar recalled. Sitting near two individuals discussing an unconfirmed ceasefire, she noticed the idea unsettled them. They questioned the timing and purpose of the pause. Once Trump’s post appeared, it became the focal point of the crowd. Daneshvar argued with a friend who dismissed the statement as an empty claim; she countered that his tone was noticeably different this time.

Minutes later, news networks ran breaking news alerts, followed by an official statement from the Supreme National Security Council. A dominant consensus then emerged across the discussion circles: they were not leaving the square. “Just as we stood alongside our military forces until now, we will stand alongside our diplomats to ensure that the conditions set are fulfilled,” many argued, according to Daneshvar.

Sara Aghili, a 29-year-old political science graduate, offered a broader interpretation of the night. “If there is a confrontation with an external enemy, it is not only the military that is engaged,” she said. “People see their presence, holding flags, gathering in the streets, as a form of participation in that struggle.”

It’s difficult to gauge where all Iranians stand on the ceasefire, as many who are anti-government are too nervous to speak to reporters. Iran and Washington appeared to have different interpretations of what was agreed to as a basis for negotiations. By Wednesday afternoon, reports of a massive Israeli attack on Lebanon began circulating, raising the probability of renewed escalation. It was later reported that Iran had again closed the Strait of Hormuz in response to Israel’s attacks on Lebanon.

Here in Tehran, Mohammadreza, a 39-year-old mechanic, adopted a cautious stance. “We have to wait and see what happens,” he said. “For now, people should stay in the streets. It’s too early to make any final judgment.”

Hanieh Qasemian is an Iranian journalist based between Iran and Lebanon. She also studies Palestinian Studies at the University of Tehran. Her work focuses on Middle Eastern affairs, militant groups, and political Islam.

Editor’s note: This article was published in collaboration with Egab. For those who asked Zeteo to protect their identity so they could speak freely, only first names were included.

Check out more from Zeteo:

item.title: 🇮🇷 🇺🇸 Did Iran Just Win This War?
item.description: On this day in 2018, Hungarian Prime Minister Viktor Orbán won the general election by a landslide, securing his third consecutive term in office. The far-right authoritarian is up for reelection once again, and despite receiving support from Donald Trump and JD Vance, who traveled to Budapest this…
item.author: First Draft | Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
On this day in 2018, Hungarian Prime Minister Viktor Orbán won the general election by a landslide, securing his third consecutive term in office. The far-right authoritarian is up for reelection once again, and despite receiving support from Donald Trump and JD Vance, who traveled to Budapest this week, Orbán’s chances aren’t looking as promising this time around.

Good morning! Andrew, Swin, and Prem here, with some decent news. Donald Trump opted not to try to slaughter Iran’s “whole civilization” Tuesday evening, as he had threatened. In Trump’s disturbing second term, we must take wins where we can. Who knows how long the “ceasefire” will last, but we, of course, welcome it: No more civilians should have to die just because our president is a raging idiot, sociopath, and pawn of Benjamin Netanyahu.

In today’s ‘First Draft,’ the ceasefire framework appears to be “a strategic win” for Iran, and a major loss for Trump and Netanyahu; liberals expand their majority on Wisconsin’s Supreme Court; and Janet Mills goes dark on the air with two months left in Maine’s Senate primary.

Don Backs Down

Donald Trump on Tuesday threatened to destroy Iran’s “whole civilization” unless its leaders agreed to a deal to reopen the Strait of Hormuz.

By Tuesday evening – instead of trying to kill the 90 million people living in Iran, or bombing Iran’s power plants and bridges as he had promised – Trump announced “a double sided CEASEFIRE!” for two weeks. In doing so, Trump all but admitted the reality that his illegal war is going terribly – and Iran is winning.

“We received a 10-point proposal from Iran, and believe it is a workable basis on which to negotiate,” the president announced on Truth Social – apparently referring to the 10-point plan that Iran had presented to the US before he threatened to murder its entire population.

Using this 10-point plan as a framework for a deal, rather than the previous American 15-point plan, is obviously a major win for Iran – which is, indeed, declaring victory. Experts and commentators see it that way, too.

“If Iran did in fact secure guarantees based on the ‘10 principles’ Trump referenced, that is not a marginal outcome, it is a strategic win for Tehran, reinforcing its narrative and positioning,” wrote Danny Citrinowicz, a former top Israeli intelligence officer who focused on Iran. As former US State Department official and veteran Middle East negotiator Aaron David Miller put it, “Iran has won another round.”

Political scientist Robert A. Pape writes: “Huge strategic defeat for the US, biggest loss since Vietnam.”

The 10-point plan would allow Iran to continue its control over the Strait of Hormuz, the crucial waterway that Iran has closed since the start of the war, stopping the flow of oil and throttling the global economy.

Just a day earlier, Trump was fantasizing about the US “charging tolls” for ships to transit the strait. Now, it looks much more likely that Iran will keep doing this instead, and that it will get to charge “a $2M fee per ship.” In another win for Iran, the plan would involve lifting “all US sanctions on Iran.”

Some liberals will invariably revel in “TACO Trump” memes about how Trump always chickens out. But, as we reported at Zeteo on Tuesday, none of this is over. The ceasefire, as of now, is only temporary. As Trump advisers and others in the upper tiers of Trumplandia warned us, there’s still a decent chance that he escalates the war again soon.

Here’s why.

“A problem is Donald Trump hates being humiliated,” one senior administration official told Swin on Tuesday. “He is going to see coverage that he was beat by the Iranians. I am expecting that to change his thinking.”

This official – who thinks the war is dragging down this presidency – also told Swin that they are trying to get a bunch of “positive” media coverage of Trump’s latest move placed in front of the president in the coming days, in order to soothe his ego and maybe (just maybe) halt an Iran-war apocalypse. This source said they are also going to talk to their contacts in right-wing media and MAGA-influencer spheres to see what kind of content those media figures are generating on Trump’s alleged “win.”

Yet, the idea that Trump “won” will be hard to support – given that he, after threatening to wipe out Iran, apparently accepted Iran’s proposal as a framework for negotiating an end to the war.

And nothing about this war can be construed as a win for the US. The war itself shouldn’t be happening. It began with a heinous war crime that killed more than 150 schoolgirls; saw the US attack a defenseless, uninvolved Iranian naval ship; and has featured numerous strikes on Iran’s civilian infrastructure (with Iran targeting civilian infrastructure around the region, in kind).

At least 13 US soldiers have died, and hundreds have been injured. Over 1,900 Iranians have been killed. And people all around the world are paying significantly higher energy prices due to this reckless war, which is expected to cause food shortages and fuel shortages. The war will likely make essential molecules scarce, which could cause another supply-chain crisis like the one we saw from COVID-19… or worse.

The attack on Iran was based on lies, it was completely unnecessary, and no one wanted it – other than Trump and Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu, who reportedly sold the US president on a rosy and deeply misguided vision of how the war would play out, per a deep dive from the New York Times on Tuesday. (Netanyahu got to brief Trump in the White House Situation Room, and Trump opted not to sit at the head of the table as he did so.)

If Tuesday night’s ceasefire was an admission of defeat from Trump, it was a much bigger loss for Netanyahu.

According to Israeli journalist Suleiman Maswadeh, Israel was “surprised” by Trump’s ceasefire announcement Tuesday night, with a source telling him: “We received updates in the final moments when everything seemed finalized. We are committed to the ceasefire.”

As Israeli analyst Mairav Zonszein noted, if the war ends this way, it will appear exceptionally pointless to Israeli civilians: “For now it looks like Israelis have been in shelters for 5 weeks in order to reopen Strait of Hormuz that was open before the war and to degrade some capabilities that can be built up within weeks.”

The 10-point framework reportedly includes Lebanon, where Israel has been leading another vicious ethnic-cleansing and mass-displacement campaign… though Israel says it won’t stop attacking the country. Iran continued to launch attacks on Israel after the tentative ceasefire was announced. Kuwait and the UAE also reported intercepting Iranian drones shortly after Trump’s announcement. Iranian state media, meanwhile, said an oil refinery on the country’s Lavan Island was struck hours after the news of the ceasefire.

We don’t know yet whether this ceasefire will last, and how or whether the war in Iran will end. At least Trump backed down, for now.

🇮🇷 Iran War Updates

Death tolls:

Iran: Iran’s deputy health minister told Al Jazeera that at least 1,937 people had been killed in US-Israeli attacks. Iranian officials said last month that nearly 20,000 people had been injured.

Lebanon: Israel has killed more than 1,530 people, including at least 130 children, in Lebanon, according to figures from the country’s Health Ministry.

US troops: At least 13 US troops have died in incidents related to the war.

Israel: At least 33 people, including 10 Israeli soldiers, have been killed and thousands injured in Iranian and Hezbollah attacks.

Elsewhere in the region: Dozens of others have been killed across the region, including four Palestinian women in the occupied West Bank.

And in more Iran war news…

Vance’s moment: Vice President JD Vance, who was reportedly brought in late Tuesday to help facilitate the 11th-hour agreement, called the ceasefire “a fragile truce.” He said some Iranian officials were misrepresenting the agreement, adding that while Trump told his negotiating team to come to the table, “if the Iranians don’t do the exact same thing, they’re going to find out that the president of the United States is not one to mess around.”

Oil prices plunge: The price of Brent crude plunged to $95 a barrel on Wednesday, a 13% decrease from before the ceasefire was announced. Though as the New York Times noted, the price per barrel is still around 30% higher than it was before Trump launched the war.

Kharg Island struck: Ahead of Trump’s ceasefire announcement, the US unleashed a new wave of strikes on Kharg Island, a five-mile-long coral outcrop in the Persian Gulf that supports roughly 90% of Iran’s crude oil exports.

Synagogue ‘completely destroyed’: US-Israeli strikes “completely destroyed” the Rafi-Nia Synagogue in Tehran yesterday, during Passover. “Our Torah scrolls were left under the rubble,” a Jewish representative in the country’s Islamic Consultative Assembly said.

Lebanon latest: After saying the two-week ceasefire would not extend to Lebanon, Israel hit the southern city of Tyre, while announcing new “evacuation” orders for several neighborhoods of Beirut.

American journalist freed: After a week in captivity, American journalist Shelly Kittleson was released yesterday by Iran-backed paramilitary group Kata’ib Hezbollah. In a statement, the group said that she would be released “on the condition that she leaves the country immediately.”

Bad-Faith Bari Buddy Battered

Michigan Democratic primary Senate candidate Abdul El-Sayed absolutely destroyed Bari Weiss employee and bad-faith Free Press reporter Olivia Reingold, after she asked him whether he supports Israel’s “right to exist as a Jewish state.”

💬 Quote Unquote

“It’s bullshit.”

That’s apparently how even the hawkish Secretary of State Marco Rubio described the Iran regime change scenarios that Benjamin Netanyahu pitched to Donald Trump back in mid-February.

🗞️ What You Need to Know

Another ICE shooting: ICE shot and injured a man in Northern California yesterday. Acting ICE Director Todd Lyons claimed, without providing evidence, that the man was a gang member wanted in El Salvador. It comes just a day after security footage from a separate ICE shooting in Minneapolis revealed that officers lied under oath.

Replacing MTG: Trump-backed candidate Clay Fuller came out victorious over Democrat Shawn Harris in the runoff election to succeed former Rep. Marjorie Taylor Greene. While the GOP may retain this seat, the race still showed a major overperformance for Democrats.

Blue wave rising: Liberals will now hold a 5-2 majority on the Wisconsin Supreme Court, after Chris Taylor won an easy victory last night.

Same old DHS: New Homeland Security Secretary Markwayne Mullin is threatening to halt customs processing for international travelers at major US airports in “sanctuary cities” like New York and Los Angeles. The move could block nearly all international travel to these airports just months ahead of the this year’s World Cup.

Maine latest: With two months left in the Senate primary, Governor Janet Mills doesn’t have any more ads booked to run on broadcast TV. As oyster farmer Graham Platner pivots to the general election, he’ll start running this new ad featuring an endorsement from a lobsterman who’s voted for Susan Collins.

🧠 Pop Quiz!

How many members of Congress called for Trump to be impeached or removed from office via the 25th Amendment yesterday?

Keep your eyes out for the answer below!

🌏 Anywhere But America

🇵🇸 Don’t forget Gaza: Israel provided cover as the Israeli-backed Abu Nasira militia launched an attack on the Maghazi refugee camp yesterday, Drop Site News reported.

🇵🇸 Beaten to death: Sixty-eight-year-old Sabriya Shamasneh was beaten to death by Israeli soldiers who raided her family home in Jayyous during the early hours of Tuesday morning. It is the latest incident of escalating Israeli violence in the occupied West Bank.

🇹🇷🇮🇱 Attack outside Israeli consulate: Gunmen opened fire near the building housing the Israeli consulate in Istanbul, leading to a gunfight with security services. The three gunmen were killed in what both Israel and Turkey declared a “terror” attack.

🇦🇺🇦🇫 Veteran charged with war crimes: Ben Roberts-Smith, one of Australia’s most decorated living veterans, was charged with five counts of war crime murder for allegedly killing five unarmed people while serving in Afghanistan from 2009 to 2012.

🇻🇳 Communist Party chief elected as president: Vietnam’s National Assembly unanimously elected Communist Party Secretary General To Lam to a five-year term as president. Many critics view the move as a dangerous step toward authoritarianism, as Lam has now consolidated his control over both the party and the country.

📊 Chart of the Day

Even when 60% of Americans disapprove of Israel, the majority of our lawmakers will still likely continue to send them billions of dollars year after year.

(See the full polling results from Pew here.)

🧠 Trivia answer: At least 86 members of Congress.

ICYMI From Zeteo

Zeteo’s Melanie Riehl and Akshay Gokul contributed to this newsletter.

Still want to get Zeteo content, but not this newsletter? You can unsubscribe directly from ‘First Draft’ by going to ‘manage subscription’ in Substack, scrolling down, and toggling the button for ‘First Draft’ off.

Vance’s moment: Vice President JD Vance, who was reportedly brought in late Tuesday to help facilitate the 11th-hour agreement, called the ceasefire “a fragile truce.” He said some Iranian officials were misrepresenting the agreement, adding that while Trump told his negotiating team to come to the table, “if the Iranians don’t do the exact same thing, they’re going to find out that the president of the United States is not one to mess around.”
